class_schedule.topic: What Are Design Systems and Why They Matter
learning_objectives: Define design systems and distinguish them from style guides and pattern libraries
Analyze existing design systems (Material Design, Apple HIG, Polaris) for structure and scope
Articulate the business case for investing in a design system
readings: Design Systems by Alla Kholmatova, Ch. 1-3
Atomic Design by Brad Frost, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: A design system is a collection of reusable components, guided by clear standards, that can be assembled to build any number of applications. It is the single source of truth for product design and development.
key_concepts: Design system components: foundations, tokens, components, patterns, documentation
Style guide vs. pattern library vs. design system: the maturity spectrum
Atomic design: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, pages
The ROI of design systems: faster development, fewer inconsistencies, easier onboarding
Governance: who owns the system and how decisions are made
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploring three production design systems — Material Design, Shopify Polaris, and Atlassian — comparing their structure, documentation, and governance models
setup_requirements: Access to public design system documentation sites

review.value: Style guide (visual standards only) < Pattern library (reusable UI patterns) < Design system (components + tokens + guidelines + governance + tooling) — each level adds consistency and scale.
Hierarchy from atoms (button) to molecules (search bar) to organisms (header) to templates (page layout) to pages (final implementation) — provides a shared vocabulary for component complexity.

learning_objectives: Create design tokens for color, typography, spacing, and elevation
Implement token naming conventions that scale across themes and platforms
Build a token architecture that supports dark mode, white-labeling, and responsive scaling
readings: Design Tokens W3C Community Group specification

lecture_notes.introduction: Design tokens are the atoms of a design system — named values that store visual design decisions. They are the bridge between design files and code, enabling consistent implementation across platforms.
key_concepts: Token types: color, typography, spacing, elevation, border-radius, opacity
Token hierarchy: global tokens > alias tokens > component tokens
Naming conventions: semantic naming (color-primary) vs. scale naming (blue-500)
Multi-theme support: light mode, dark mode, high contrast through token swapping
Token formats: Figma variables, JSON, CSS custom properties, platform-native
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete token system in Figma Variables — defining global color palette, creating semantic aliases, and switching between light and dark themes

review.value: Global (blue-500: #2563EB) > Alias (color-primary: blue-500) > Component (button-bg: color-primary) — three levels enable theme switching by only changing alias mappings.
Name tokens by their purpose, not their value: color-success not green-500, spacing-md not 16px — semantic names survive redesigns where values change but purposes remain.

learning_objectives: Design components with clear APIs: props, variants, slots, and states
Build component variants that cover all necessary states and sizes
Create compound components from smaller atomic parts
readings: Component-Driven User Interfaces by Brad Frost (article)
Figma documentation: Component properties and variants

lecture_notes.introduction: Components are the building blocks of a design system. Their API — what can be configured and how — determines whether they are flexible enough to cover real use cases without becoming unmanageably complex.
key_concepts: Component API: properties that consumers can configure
Variant management: boolean, enum, and instance-swap properties
State coverage: default, hover, focus, active, disabled, error, loading
Compound components: assembling larger patterns from smaller components
Component boundaries: when to create a new component vs. add a variant
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a Button component in Figma with full API: size (sm/md/lg), variant (primary/secondary/ghost), icon position (left/right/none), loading state, and disabled state

review.value: A component's API is like a function signature — it defines what consumers can configure. Good APIs are minimal (few required props), flexible (cover real use cases), and constrained (prevent misuse).
Every interactive component needs at minimum: default, hover, focus (keyboard), active (pressed), disabled, and loading states. Missing states create accessibility gaps and inconsistent behavior.

learning_objectives: Write component documentation that serves both designers and developers
Design contribution workflows for adding and modifying system components
Create governance models that balance consistency with team autonomy
readings: Design Systems by Alla Kholmatova, Ch. 7-8
InVision Design Better: Design System Governance

lecture_notes.introduction: A design system is only as good as its documentation. If people cannot find, understand, and correctly use components, the system fails regardless of how well-designed the components are.
key_concepts: Documentation anatomy: usage guidelines, do/don't examples, code snippets, accessibility notes
Contribution models: centralized, federated, and hybrid governance
Versioning and change management: breaking vs. non-breaking changes
Adoption metrics: how to measure design system usage and impact
Deprecation workflows: retiring components without breaking products
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing complete documentation for a Card component — usage guidelines, anatomy diagram, variant table, do/don't examples, accessibility requirements, and code implementation notes
setup_requirements: Documentation platform (Storybook, Zeroheight, or custom site)

review.value: Product teams contribute components back to the system through a review process — balances team autonomy with system consistency. Works best when clear contribution guidelines exist.
Visual examples showing correct and incorrect component usage are more effective than written rules alone — designers remember examples better than instructions.

learning_objectives: Develop strategies for driving design system adoption across an organization
Measure design system health and impact using appropriate metrics
Plan for design system evolution including major version upgrades
readings: Design System Handbook by InVision, Ch. on Adoption
Measuring Design System Success (article by Sparkbox)

lecture_notes.introduction: Building a design system is the easy part. Getting teams to adopt it, maintaining it over time, and evolving it as the product grows are the real challenges.
key_concepts: Adoption strategies: evangelism, training, migration support, quick wins
Adoption metrics: component coverage, design-to-code consistency, team satisfaction
Health metrics: contribution rate, issue resolution time, documentation currency
Migration planning: moving existing products to the design system incrementally
Major version upgrades: handling breaking changes without breaking products
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating an adoption dashboard — tracking design system usage metrics across multiple product teams and identifying adoption barriers
setup_requirements: Analytics tracking for Figma library usage

review.value: Common barriers: components don't fit real use cases, documentation is outdated, contribution process is too slow, teams don't know the system exists. Each requires a different strategy.
Percentage of UI elements in production that use design system components vs. custom implementations — the primary metric for design system adoption.

questions.question: Explain the three-level token hierarchy (global, alias, component) and demonstrate how it enables theme switching with a color example.
questions.answer: Three levels: Global tokens define raw values (blue-500: #2563EB, blue-800: #1E40AF). Alias tokens map semantic purposes to global values (color-primary: blue-500). Component tokens reference aliases (button-bg: color-primary). Theme switching works by only changing alias mappings: in dark mode, color-primary maps to blue-300 instead of blue-500. All components referencing color-primary automatically update because the alias changed — no component modifications needed.

rubric.key: Correctly defines all three token levels

questions.question: A team names their spacing tokens spacing-4, spacing-8, spacing-16, spacing-24. Another team uses spacing-xs, spacing-sm, spacing-md, spacing-lg. Compare these approaches and recommend which is better for a design system.
questions.answer: Numeric naming (spacing-4, spacing-8) ties the name to the value, which means if the spacing scale changes, the names become misleading (spacing-8 might map to 10px after a redesign). Semantic/t-shirt naming (spacing-xs, spacing-sm) describes relative purpose, surviving value changes. However, numeric names are more precise when designers need to reason about exact values. Recommendation: use semantic names as the primary API (spacing-sm, spacing-md) because they survive redesigns, but document the current pixel values clearly. This matches how color tokens work — color-primary is better than blue-500 for the same reason.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the fragility of numeric naming
Correctly identifies the resilience of semantic naming
Provides a clear recommendation with reasoning

questions.question: A product team needs a 'UserCard' component that shows a user avatar, name, role, and an action button. Should this be a new component in the design system, or assembled from existing atoms? Explain your reasoning.
questions.answer: It should be assembled from existing atoms: Avatar component + Text atoms (name, role) + Button component, composed into a molecule-level layout. Adding UserCard as a design system component is premature because: (1) It is product-specific — not all products need user cards. (2) It can be composed from existing system components without new design system code. (3) Design systems should provide building blocks, not every possible assembly. Exception: if 3+ product teams independently build similar user cards, promoting it to the system reduces duplication. The decision criterion is reuse count, not complexity.

rubric.key: Recommends composition over new system component
Explains why product-specific patterns stay outside the system
Identifies the reuse threshold for system promotion

questions.question: Describe the difference between centralized and federated design system governance. What are the trade-offs of each, and when is each appropriate?
questions.answer: Centralized: a dedicated design system team owns all components and makes all decisions. Trade-offs: high consistency and quality, but slow response to product team needs, bottleneck risk, and components may not fit edge cases. Appropriate for: organizations with a small number of products and a need for strict brand consistency. Federated: product teams contribute components through a shared review process. Trade-offs: faster response to needs and broader ownership, but risk of inconsistency and quality variation. Appropriate for: large organizations with many product teams and diverse needs. Most mature systems use a hybrid: core team maintains foundations and governance, product teams contribute components through a defined process.

rubric.key: Correct definition of centralized with trade-offs
Correct definition of federated with trade-offs

major_assignments.title: Design System Creation Project
major_assignments.description: Build a production-quality design system for a real or hypothetical multi-product organization. Deliver a complete token system, a component library with at least 15 components, comprehensive documentation, and a governance proposal.
objectives: Create a scalable design token architecture with theme support
Build a component library covering foundational through complex components
Write documentation that serves both designers and developers
Propose a governance model for maintaining and evolving the system
deliverables: Token system: color, typography, spacing, elevation with light and dark themes
Component library: 15+ components with full state and variant coverage in Figma
Documentation site or document: usage guidelines, examples, and accessibility notes
Governance proposal: contribution process, versioning, and adoption strategy
Demo: two sample screens built entirely from system components

criteria: Complete token architecture with global, alias, and component levels
Theme support (light/dark) through token swapping
Naming conventions are semantic and consistent
Token documentation is clear and developer-friendly
15+ components covering atoms through organisms
Full state coverage: default, hover, focus, active, disabled, error, loading
Component APIs are well-designed with appropriate props and variants
Components compose correctly into larger patterns
Every component has usage guidelines with do/don't examples
Accessibility requirements documented for each component
Documentation format is clear and navigable
Code implementation guidance included
Contribution process is clear and realistic
Versioning and change management strategy defined
Adoption metrics and measurement plan included
Evolution roadmap addresses future needs
Scalable token architecture with theme support
Comprehensive component library with full state coverage
Professional documentation serving designers and developers
Realistic governance proposal with adoption strategy

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on design system architecture, token systems, and governance models

system_labs.description: Weekly labs building design system components with increasing complexity

design_system_project.description: Complete design system with tokens, components, documentation, and governance

system_critiques.description: Peer review and critique of classmates' design system components

final_presentation.description: Presentation of design system to a panel of design and engineering professionals

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions involve collaborative component building and review. Attendance expected for all sessions. More than 3 absences affect participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Component deliverables build sequentially. Late submissions penalized 10% per day and miss peer review opportunities.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review is required. All design system work must be individual. Sharing Figma component libraries is prohibited for graded work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Design systems may reference existing public systems for patterns and inspiration. Direct copying of component implementations without attribution is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma with Variables and component features, documentation platform access (Zeroheight free tier or custom), and browser for testing component usage.
